title: Binder, Services & IPC
description: Binder driver architecture, AIDL, Parcel serialization, service binding, Messenger, ContentProvider as IPC, and PendingIntent security
lesson:
Why Binder Exists

Every Android app runs in its own Linux process with its own memory space. You cannot share objects across processes — memory addresses are meaningless to another process. IPC (Inter-Process Communication) is required for any cross-process interaction: calling a system service, binding to another app's service, or even starting an Activity.

Why Binder over pipes/sockets/shared memory?
• Single-copy transfer — data is copied once from sender to kernel, then mapped into receiver's address space (vs two copies for pipes/sockets)
• Identity propagation — the kernel attaches the calling process's UID/PID, so the server can verify who's calling (security!)
• Object reference model — Binder objects have identity; you can pass references, not just data
• Thread pool model — each process has a Binder thread pool (~16 threads) ready to handle incoming calls

Binder Architecture

Your App Process System Server / Other App ┌──────────────┐ ┌──────────────┐ │ Client Code │ │ Real Service │ │ │ │ │ ▲ │ │ ▼ │ │ │ │ │ Proxy Stub │ │ Stub (Bn) │ │ (Bp) │ │ ▲ │ │ │ │ │ │ │ │ Parcel │ │ Parcel │ │ serialize │ │ deserialize│ └──────┬───────┘ └──────┴───────┘ │ ┌─────────────┐ │ └────────▶│ /dev/binder │◀─────────┘ │ (kernel) │ │ single-copy │ │ transfer │ └─────────────┘

The flow: (1) Client calls proxy method → (2) Proxy serializes args into a Parcel → (3) ioctl() sends Parcel to kernel Binder driver → (4) Kernel copies data to server process → (5) Server stub deserializes → (6) Calls real method → (7) Response follows reverse path.

Parcel & Serialization

Parcel is Binder's serialization container. It's a byte buffer optimized for IPC — not for persistent storage.

ParcelableSerializable
Android-specific interfaceJava standard interface
Explicit read/write order — fastUses reflection — 10x slower
@Parcelize auto-generatesJust add : Serializable
Use for IPC & BundlesAvoid on Android
// @Parcelize generates writeToParcel/createFromParcel @Parcelize data class User( val id: String, val name: String, val email: String ) : Parcelable
1MB Transaction Limit: The Binder driver limits each transaction to ~1MB (shared across the entire process). Sending a large Bitmap or huge list via Intent/Bundle will crash with TransactionTooLargeException. For large data, use ContentProvider, FileProvider, or shared memory.

AIDL: Defining IPC Interfaces

AIDL (Android Interface Definition Language) generates the proxy/stub boilerplate for Binder IPC:

// IRemoteService.aidl interface IRemoteService { int getProcessId(); oneway void logMessage(String msg); // async — returns immediately User getUser(in String userId); // 'in' = input only } // Generated: IRemoteService.Stub (server implements) // Generated: IRemoteService.Stub.Proxy (client calls)
ModifierMeaning
inParameter sent to server (default)
outParameter filled by server, returned to client
inoutSent both ways (expensive — avoid if possible)
onewayAsync call — returns immediately, no return value, no exceptions propagated back
When to use what:
• AIDL — Need fine-grained IPC with multiple methods, types, callbacks
• Messenger — Simple message passing, single-threaded handler, no concurrency
• ContentProvider — URI-based data access, CRUD operations, cursor results

Service Binding Mechanics

Client Activity AMS (system_server) Service Process │ │ │ │── bindService() ───────────▶ │ │ │ │── startProcess (if needed) ─▶│ │ │ │── onCreate() │ │◀── onBind() returns IBinder ─│ │◀── onServiceConnected() ─────│ │ │ (receives proxy) │ │ │ │ │ │── proxy.getUser() ──────────────── Binder IPC ─────────────▶│── real getUser() │◀── result ──────────────────────── Binder IPC ──────────────│
TypeLifecycleUse Case
Started (startService)Lives until stopSelf() or stopService()Background work (music, download)
Bound (bindService)Lives while any client is boundIPC, client-server interaction
Started+BoundLives until stopped AND all unboundMusic player (plays independently, UI binds to control)

PendingIntent Security

A PendingIntent wraps your identity and an Intent. When another app fires it, it executes with your app's permissions, not theirs.

// SAFE: FLAG_IMMUTABLE prevents modification val pi = PendingIntent.getActivity( context, 0, intent, PendingIntent.FLAG_IMMUTABLE or PendingIntent.FLAG_UPDATE_CURRENT ) // DANGEROUS: FLAG_MUTABLE lets the sender modify the Intent // Only use when you need fillIn() for inline reply, bubbles, etc. val mutablePi = PendingIntent.getActivity( context, 0, intent, PendingIntent.FLAG_MUTABLE )
Android 12+ requirement: You must explicitly specify either FLAG_IMMUTABLE or FLAG_MUTABLE. Omitting both crashes. Always prefer FLAG_IMMUTABLE unless you need the sender to fill in extras (e.g., notification inline reply).

ContentProvider as IPC

ContentProvider uses Binder underneath. It exposes data via URIs and supports standard CRUD operations cross-process. Each query result is a Cursor backed by a shared memory window (CursorWindow), but the window is limited to ~2MB.

// ContentProvider URI structure content://com.example.provider/users/42 │ │ │ │ scheme authority path id // Efficient batch operations val ops = ArrayList<ContentProviderOperation>() ops.add(ContentProviderOperation.newInsert(CONTENT_URI) .withValue("name", "Alice").build()) ops.add(ContentProviderOperation.newInsert(CONTENT_URI) .withValue("name", "Bob").build()) contentResolver.applyBatch(AUTHORITY, ops) // Single Binder transaction!

questions.title: Why Binder Over Standard IPC
questions.description: Why does Android use Binder for IPC instead of standard Linux mechanisms like pipes or Unix domain sockets?
options: Binder is faster because it uses UDP instead of TCP
Binder provides single-copy data transfer and automatic caller identity propagation (UID/PID) — essential for Android's security model
Binder works without a kernel — it's a userspace-only mechanism
Binder is simpler to implement than pipes
questions.explanation: Binder has two critical advantages: (1) Single-copy transfer — data is copied once from sender into kernel-mapped memory that the receiver can read directly (pipes/sockets copy twice: sender→kernel→receiver). (2) Identity propagation — the kernel automatically attaches the calling process's UID and PID to each transaction, so system services can enforce permissions without trusting the caller's self-reported identity. This is the foundation of Android's entire permission system.
hints: Think about security: how does a system service know WHO is calling it?
Standard IPC copies data twice — Binder optimizes this

questions.title: bindService() Call Trace
questions.description: Trace what happens when your Activity calls bindService(intent, connection, BIND_AUTO_CREATE). Which processes are involved and what travels over Binder?
options: bindService() directly calls the service's onBind() in the same process

bindService() sends a Binder transaction to AMS in system_server, which starts the service process (if needed), calls onBind() to get an IBinder, then delivers it back to the client via onServiceConnected() — three processes involved
bindService() uses shared memory to connect the two processes without any system service involvement
bindService() creates a socket connection between the two processes
questions.explanation: Three processes participate: (1) Your app calls bindService() which sends a Binder transaction to system_server. (2) AMS (ActivityManagerService) in system_server starts the service process if needed, then calls its onBind(). (3) The service process returns an IBinder from onBind(). AMS delivers this IBinder back to your app's ServiceConnection.onServiceConnected(). After that, your proxy calls go directly to the service via Binder — AMS is no longer in the path.
hints: AMS mediates the initial connection but not subsequent calls
BIND_AUTO_CREATE means 'start the service if it's not running'

questions.title: AIDL Interface Definition
questions.description: Fill in the blanks to define an AIDL interface for a music player service.
questions.code: // IMusicService.aidl ___1___ IMusicService { void play(___2___ String trackId); void pause(); ___3___ void reportAnalytics(String event); boolean isPlaying(); }

blanks.hint: AIDL keyword to define an IPC interface
Direction modifier: parameter sent to server only
Modifier for async calls that return immediately

questions.explanation: AIDL uses interface to define Binder contracts. in means the parameter is sent to the server (default for primitives, explicit for objects). oneway makes the call asynchronous — the client doesn't wait for a result and no exceptions propagate back. Use oneway for fire-and-forget calls like analytics logging.
hints: AIDL syntax looks like Java interfaces with IPC-specific modifiers
reportAnalytics doesn't need a result — it can be async

questions.title: Binder Transaction Limit
questions.description: What is the Binder transaction buffer size limit, and what happens when you exceed it?
options: 10MB limit — the system logs a warning but continues
~1MB total per process — exceeding it throws TransactionTooLargeException and can crash the app
256KB per transaction — the system silently truncates extra data
No limit — Binder automatically uses shared memory for large payloads
questions.explanation: The Binder transaction buffer is ~1MB per process, shared across all concurrent transactions. Sending a large Bitmap, huge list, or oversized Bundle via Intent/startActivity/onSaveInstanceState can exceed this. TransactionTooLargeException is thrown, often crashing the app. Solutions: pass URIs instead of data, use FileProvider, ContentProvider, or shared memory (Ashmem). This is also why the saved instance state Bundle should only contain small, essential state.
hints: This is the same limit that affects SavedStateHandle Bundles
The buffer is shared across ALL concurrent Binder transactions in the process

questions.title: PendingIntent Security Vulnerability
questions.description: This notification PendingIntent has a security vulnerability on Android 12+. Additionally, a malicious app receiving this mutable PendingIntent could modify the Intent to launch a different Activity. Fix it.

checks.message: Use FLAG_IMMUTABLE to prevent Intent modification

questions.explanation: Android 12+ requires either FLAG_IMMUTABLE or FLAG_MUTABLE — omitting both throws an exception. FLAG_IMMUTABLE prevents anyone who receives the PendingIntent from modifying the wrapped Intent. With FLAG_MUTABLE, a malicious app could change the target component or extras. Always use FLAG_IMMUTABLE unless you specifically need mutability (e.g., notification inline reply which needs fillIn() to add the user's typed text).
hints: Android 12 made this flag mandatory for a reason — security
A PendingIntent executes with YOUR app's permissions, not the sender's

questions.title: Parcelable vs Serializable
questions.description: Why does Android strongly prefer Parcelable over Serializable for IPC?
options: Parcelable encrypts data during IPC, Serializable does not
Parcelable uses explicit, hand-written serialization (no reflection) — roughly 10x faster than Serializable's reflection-based approach
Parcelable supports larger data sizes than Serializable
Parcelable works across different programming languages, Serializable is Java-only
questions.explanation: Serializable uses Java reflection to discover fields, their types, and their values — this is slow and creates temporary objects. Parcelable has explicit writeToParcel() and createFromParcel() methods with a defined read/write order — no reflection, no extra allocations, ~10x faster. With @Parcelize, the Kotlin compiler generates this code automatically, giving you Parcelable's speed with Serializable's convenience.
hints: Reflection is the key difference — it's slow and creates garbage
@Parcelize removes the boilerplate argument against Parcelable

questions.title: AIDL oneway Semantics
questions.description: How does the oneway keyword change the behavior of this AIDL method?
options: oneway makes trackEvent() run on the main thread instead of a Binder thread

oneway makes trackEvent() asynchronous — the client returns immediately without waiting for the server to finish; no return value is possible and exceptions in the server are NOT propagated back to the client
oneway means the data is only sent from client to server, never from server to client
oneway encrypts the transaction for security
questions.explanation: oneway makes the Binder call non-blocking: the client sends the transaction and immediately returns without waiting for the server to process it. Consequences: (1) The method must return void. (2) If the server throws an exception, the client never knows. (3) Multiple oneway calls from the same client are queued and executed in order. getEventCount() without oneway is synchronous — the client blocks until the server returns the int.
hints: Think about the difference between fire-and-forget vs request-response
oneway is great for logging/analytics where you don't need a result

questions.title: Bound Service Implementation
questions.description: Implement a bound service that exposes a method getServerTime(): Long using a Binder subclass (same-process binding). Show both the Service and the client Activity's ServiceConnection.
questions.explanation: For same-process binding, extend Binder directly (no AIDL needed). The inner class pattern lets the client get a reference to the Service itself. Key lifecycle: bind in onStart(), unbind in onStop() — this prevents leaks. BIND_AUTO_CREATE starts the service if it's not running. For cross-process IPC, you'd use AIDL or Messenger instead of a local Binder.
hints: For same-process binding, AIDL is overkill — a Binder subclass works
Always unbind in the opposite lifecycle method to prevent leaks

questions.title: Service Lifecycle: Started vs Bound
questions.description: A service is both started (via startService()) and bound (via bindService()). When is it destroyed?
options: When stopService() is called — binding doesn't matter
When all clients unbind — starting doesn't matter
When BOTH stopService() is called AND all clients have unbound
Never — started+bound services run forever
questions.explanation: A started+bound service has two lifecycle anchors. It only gets destroyed when both conditions are met: (1) stopService() or stopSelf() has been called, AND (2) all clients have unbound. This is common for music players: the service is started (keeps playing when Activity leaves) and bound (Activity controls playback). It keeps running after unbind because it's still started, and keeps running after stop because clients are still bound.
hints: Think of a music player: it plays when the UI is gone (started) AND has controls when visible (bound)

questions.title: Service Connection Leak
questions.description: This Activity leaks the ServiceConnection — Android logs: "Activity has leaked ServiceConnection." Fix it.

checks.message: Unbind the service in a lifecycle method

questions.explanation: The bug: bindService() is called but unbindService() is never called. When the Activity is destroyed, the ServiceConnection leaks. Fix: (1) Move bind to onStart() and unbind to onStop() — matching lifecycle methods. (2) Track the bound flag to avoid double-unbind crashes. (3) Make dataService nullable since it's only available after connection. This is a top Android memory leak pattern.
hints: Every bindService() must have a matching unbindService()
Bind and unbind should be in matching lifecycle methods (onStart/onStop or onCreate/onDestroy)
